notes: Two profiles, deliberately different. `live_snapshot` validates ONE NEW DAY's value against current-era bounds and is consumed by fetch_snapshot.py (P2). `backfill_absolute` validates the FULL historical series (2010-present) in this phase's pytest suite and must hold for early low-price years too -- never reuse live_snapshot bounds against backfilled history, and never weaken either profile to make a check pass.

block_height.note: Not backfilled as a history file in P1 -- this rule is pinned now for P2's fetch_snapshot.py.

cross_source_variance.hashrate_pct_note: Widened from a naive tight threshold (director-reviewed correction). Hash-rate ESTIMATORS across providers use different trailing-average smoothing windows (e.g. a 3-day vs. a 7-day window) and routinely disagree by double digits even when both are 'correct'. A tight threshold here would just generate constant false-positive VARIANCE_WARN noise.

price_usd.note: Min is near-zero, not 1000 -- BTC legitimately traded under $1 through 2010-2011.

difficulty.max_pct_change_note: Left unenforced for backfill (unlike live_snapshot's 30% bound). Verified against real blockchain.info data during the P1 backfill run: 2009-2013 retargets legitimately swung 30-300%+ per period during the CPU->GPU->FPGA->ASIC hardware transitions -- real history, not corruption. A single fixed threshold can't fit both that era and the stable modern one, so this is a WARN-never check for backfilled history; min/max bounds plus the schema's exclusiveMinimum are what catch actual corruption here.
